setup_positioning: Tibial Shaft Fracture ORIF (open_approach). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.

Tibia subcutaneous anteromedially (easy landmark for approach); leg has 4 compartments (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior). IMN gold standard; plate for specific proximal/distal or soft tissue needs.
Anterior compartment: tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL, deep peroneal nerve, anterior tibial vessels. At risk in anterolateral approaches or swelling.
Lateral compartment: peroneus longus/brevis, superficial peroneal nerve (sensory to dorsum foot). Nerve exits fascia ~10-12 cm proximal to lateral malleolus.
Medial: saphenous vein and nerve (sensory) cross anteromedial distal third. At risk in medial approaches or incisions.
Posterior: superficial (gastroc/soleus/plantaris), deep (tib post, FDL, FHL, posterior tibial vessels, tibial nerve). Popliteal trifurcation proximally.
Compartment syndrome risk high in tibial shaft (swelling, bleeding); monitor pressures if tense or high-energy. Fasciotomy if indicated.

Anteromedial: along subcutaneous border (easy, but skin issues in high energy). Incision medial to crest, develop to bone avoiding saphenous. Good for medial plate or open fractures with medial wound.

Anterolateral: for lateral plate or when medial skin bad. Interval or split anterior compartment muscles; protect deep peroneal and ant tib vessels. Lateral compartment muscles (peronei) retracted posteriorly if needed.

Reduction/plating: traction for length, direct clamp or indirect for alignment. Plate on safe corridor (medial or lateral depending approach). Check rotation with contralateral or fluoro. Respect compartments throughout.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to fascia over anteromedial border or anterior/lateral compartments)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anteromedial: incision along subcutaneous border (medial to tibial crest). Develop to bone, protect saphenous vein/nerve crossing distally. Anterolateral: incision over anterior compartment, develop to fascia, identify superficial peroneal exit point distally if lateral work.
key_structures: Superficial peroneal nerve exit (lateral, ~10-12cm above malleolus)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Anteromedial is simplest for medial plating/open wounds. Anterolateral for lateral plate when medial skin compromised. Identify nerves early to prevent iatrogenic injury.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep (compartment muscles to bone, safe corridors)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Anteromedial: strip periosteum carefully from medial border. Protect saphenous. For lateral: develop anterior compartment (protect deep peroneal/ant tib v), retract peronei if needed for lateral surface. Identify safe plate corridors (medial or lateral depending approach).
key_structures: Deep peroneal n / ant tib vessels
structures_at_risk: Deep peroneal n / ant tib v (anterior)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The working layer for reduction and fixation. Neurovascular protection and compartment awareness are essential; tibial shaft has high compartment risk.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Close fascia loosely or not at all if swelling/compartment concern. Skin in layers. High vigilance for compartment syndrome post-op (pain, swelling, tense compartments) - measure pressures if uncertain. Fasciotomy if indicated (4-compartment).
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Tibial shaft fractures have high compartment syndrome risk due to swelling and bleeding into closed compartments. Loose closure and monitoring prevent disaster.

structures_at_risk.structure: Deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial vessels (anterior compartment)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: In anterior compartment; at risk during anterolateral approach, swelling, or retraction for lateral plating. Nerve supplies EHL/EDL/TA and sensation 1st web.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect with vessel loop in anterior compartment work. Limit retraction time/force. Monitor for compartment syndrome (high risk in tibial shaft).
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Foot drop (EHL/EDL/TA weakness), 1st web numbness. May be neuropraxia or ischemic if compartment missed.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Anterolateral or anterior exposure for tibial shaft plating

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Exits lateral compartment fascia ~10-12 cm above lateral malleolus; at risk in lateral or anterolateral incisions or swelling.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Know the exit point; protect during lateral compartment retraction or fasciotomy. Use direct visualization for distal lateral work.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Sensory loss dorsum foot (except 1st web). Painful neuroma possible. Motor usually spared (peronei innervated more proximally).

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Cross anteromedial distal leg; at risk in medial incisions or during saphenous vein harvest-like exposure for medial plate.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify under skin in distal medial approaches. Protect with vessel loop or retract carefully. Avoid blind clamping.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Saphenous neuralgia (medial leg/foot numbness/pain) or vein injury (bleeding, later varicosities if harvest context).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial approach for tibial shaft plating

structures_at_risk.structure: Posterior tibial vessels and tibial nerve (deep posterior compartment)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: In deep posterior; at risk with posterior swelling, missed compartment syndrome, or if posteromedial work needed for specific fractures.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Monitor compartments (high risk in tibial shaft). If posterior exposure needed, use careful plane and direct visualization. Fasciotomy if pressures elevated.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Tibial nerve deficit (intrinsic foot weakness, plantar numbness), vascular compromise to foot. Missed compartment leads to contractures (Volkmann).
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Tibial shaft with compartment concerns or posteromedial access

pitfalls: Failure to identify/protect superficial peroneal nerve at its fascial exit in lateral/anterolateral approaches, causing dorsal foot numbness.
Inadequate protection of deep peroneal/ant tib vessels during anterior compartment work, leading to foot drop or ischemic injury.
Missing compartment syndrome (high risk in tibial shaft); delayed fasciotomy leads to Volkmann contracture and severe disability.
Saphenous injury in medial approaches (neuralgia or vein damage).
Ignoring rotation/length in reduction or using unsafe plate corridors without checking neurovascular.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What are the 4 leg compartments and key contents at risk in tibial shaft fractures/ORIF? A: Anterior (TA/EHL/EDL, deep peroneal n, ant tib v); Lateral (peronei, sup peroneal n); Superficial post (gastroc/soleus); Deep post (tib post/FDL/FHL, post tib v, tibial n). All at risk for compartment syndrome.

Q: Describe the superficial peroneal nerve course and why it is at risk in lateral/anterolateral tibial approaches. A: Runs in lateral compartment, exits fascia ~10-12 cm above lateral malleolus to become subcutaneous. At risk in lateral incisions or swelling; injury causes dorsal foot numbness (motor usually spared).

Q: How do you protect the deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial vessels during anterolateral exposure for tibial plating? A: Develop anterior compartment carefully, identify and loop the neurovascular bundle, limit retraction, and monitor for compartment syndrome (high risk in these fractures).

Q: What is the saphenous nerve/vein risk in medial approaches to the tibial shaft and how to avoid? A: Cross anteromedial distal third. Identify under skin, protect with loop or careful retraction. Injury causes medial leg/foot sensory loss or neuroma.

Q: Why is compartment syndrome monitoring critical after tibial shaft ORIF and what are the consequences if missed? A: High risk due to swelling/bleeding into closed compartments. Missed leads to Volkmann contracture (severe disability, foot drop, claw toes, sensory loss). Measure pressures if tense/pain out of proportion; fasciotomy if elevated.
